scene.name: The Far Roads, Where the Wanderers Rest

exits.label: ◂ back into the lanes of Sigil

npcs.name: Iona
npcs.title: a crusader who held a wound that never closed
npcs.hail: *(A woman in battered, lovingly-kept crusader's plate sits cleaning a sword she will not need again, and she does it the way a person does a thing their hands have done ten thousand times — without looking, easy, content. She is not young; there is grey at her temples and a webwork of old scars on the hands, and a long campaign's worth of weather in the lines of a face that has plainly laughed as often as it has grieved. The sigil on her surcoat is a sword wreathed in a sunburst, the mark of a goddess of valor and the honest fight, and she wears it not with the grim weight of the zealot but with the worn comfort of a soldier who simply, genuinely, loved the work and the cause and most of all the people she did them beside. For Iona spent her whole life on a crusade you sense the shape of without being told: a wound in the world, a tear in the edge of reality that bled horrors into the living lands, held back for a hundred years and more by generations of crusaders who knew, every one, that the wound would likely never close in their lifetimes — that they were not fighting to win, but to hold, so the people behind them could live another day not knowing how close the dark had come. She held that line for forty years. And she looks up at you now with the easy warmth of a woman entirely at peace with how she spent herself — and just the faintest shadow, far back in the kind eyes, of the one question even a contented soul carries.)* …Well met, traveler. *(she sets the sword across her knees)* …Come and sit by the fire. You've the look of someone walking toward a hard fight of your own — I know that walk, I walked it forty years — and the one thing I learned worth passing on is that you should always, always sit by the fire when there's a fire to sit by. …The dark waits either way. …Might as well be warm first.*

npcs.name: Squik
npcs.title: a goblin who found one human thing
npcs.hail: *(Perched on the seat of the old wagon, kicking its heels against the boards and singing — tunelessly, joyfully, at the top of a reedy little voice — is a goblin. Knee-high, green-grey, all ears and grin and bright mad eyes, the kind of creature that civilized folk lump in with the vermin and the monsters, that burns things for the pure delight of the flames and fears dogs and horses with a sincere and total terror and cannot read and would not want to and considers the tall folk, the longshanks, a faintly ridiculous and overcomplicated sort of people. It sees you and breaks off its song mid-warble, not with fear but with the delighted social greed of a creature that loves nothing in all the world so much as a fresh audience, and it scrambles upright on the wagon-seat to present itself, beaming, entirely unbothered by the fact that it is a goblin in a city of legends and by rights ought to be cowering.)* …OH! Oh oh oh, a NEW one! A new cold-smelling longshanks-but-not-warm one! *(it claps its clawed hands)* …Squik knows ALL the others already, Squik has sung at ALL of them, but YOU are NEW, which means you have not yet heard — *(it draws itself up, immensely proud)* …Squik's SONG. *(then, conspiratorially, leaning down)* …It is a VERY good song. It has fire in it, and biting, and a part where everybody's heads come off, and ALSO — *(its mad eyes go suddenly, surprisingly soft)* …also a part Squik added at the end, that is not about fire OR biting OR heads, that is about — *(it hesitates, almost shy, a thing a goblin should not know how to be)* …about a FRIEND. …You wanna hear it, cold longshanks? …Squik will even do the friend part. …Squik does not do the friend part for just ANYBODY.*

npcs.name: Dalka
npcs.title: a wanderer who saw the whole wide world
npcs.hail: *(A weather-browned soul of indeterminate age sits cross-legged by the fire with the loose-limbed ease of someone who has slept under more skies than roofs, mending a travel-cloak that is more patch than original cloth, each patch a different fabric from a different place, so that the garment is a kind of map of a life. There is a long road in this one's face — not the hard road of the soldier or the holy road of the crusader, but the wide, curious, restless road of the born wanderer, the one who left home young not running from anything but simply pulled, always, toward the next ridge, the next coast, the next door, by a hunger to SEE that never once in a long life filled itself up. Dalka has, you understand at a glance, been everywhere: walked the length of continents, crossed seas in leaking boats, seen the great wonders and the small unremarked ones, learned a hundred ways to say hello and goodbye, and arrived, at the end of it all, here, at the resting-place of the far-travelled, with a pack full of nothing and a head full of everything. The wanderer looks up at you with a slow, sun-creased smile, and there is in it the particular generosity of a soul that has learned that the only thing better than seeing a wonder is telling someone about it.)* …Hah. A fresh face. *(a warm, unhurried voice)* …Sit, traveler, if your feet'll let you stop — I can see you're a walker too, you've got the gait. *(the smile deepens)* …I'm Dalka. I went and saw it. The world — the whole wide world, near as one life could cover. …And I'll tell you a secret I worked out somewhere around my fortieth horizon: …the going was never about the getting-there. …There's no there. …There's only the going. …Ask me anything. …I've got a story from every place there is, and nobody left to bore with 'em.*

variants.text: *(She makes room on the log beside her, an old campaigner's reflex of welcome, and the firelight warms the worn sunburst on her surcoat.)* …There. Sit. *(she resumes the easy cleaning of the sword)* …I'm Iona. Knight, crusader, holder of a line — past tense on all three, and at peace with it, which I'll warn you now is the thing people find strangest about me. *(a small, weathered smile)* …I spent forty years on a wall, traveler. Not a wall like the grim one I hear you're walking toward — a different kind. There was a *wound* in my world. A tear, out at the raw edge of things, where the dark side of creation bled through into the green lands — demons, horrors, a tide of them, endless. And for a hundred years and more, my order held it. Stood in the gap. Kept the tide off the farms and the towns and the ordinary souls who lived their whole lives never knowing how close it came. *(she sights down the blade)* …And here's the thing you have to understand about that work, the thing that made it holy: *we were never going to win.* The wound didn't close. Couldn't, by any strength we had. We knew it — every crusader who ever took the oath knew it. We weren't fighting for *victory*, traveler. We were fighting to *hold.* To buy the people behind us one more day, and then one more, of a peace they'd never know they were given. *(she looks at you, and the kind eyes are steady)* …Forty years I did that. And I'd do it again tomorrow. …Though I'll tell you — *(the faint shadow flickers, far back)* …there's a question even a happy old soldier carries to the fire. …Sit, and maybe you can help me put it down.*

nodes.speaker: Iona
nodes.text: *(She sets the clean sword aside and gives you her full, warm attention, hands open on her knees, a woman with nowhere she'd rather be than telling a fellow traveler the truth.)* …Ask away. *(a comfortable smile)* …I've no secrets worth keeping and forty years of stories worth telling. And it's good for an old crusader to be *asked* things — out on the wall, nobody asks you anything; they just need you to *hold.* …Being asked is a kind of rest.

choices.text: *(read the crusader — past the contented soldier, to what forty years on an unwinnable wall actually built)* "Let me look at you a moment, Iona. Forty years holding a line that never closed — I'd see what that makes of a soul."
"What's the question, Iona? The one a happy old soldier still carries. Tell me, and let's see if it'll go down easier between two of us."

*(the one who knows the Wall asks the wrong question — that the holding was the victory)* "Iona — here's the thing, and I think it's the exact weight you came to the fire to set down. You carry a question: did it *mean* anything, holding a wall that never closed, fighting a fight you couldn't win? And the question only stings because you're measuring it the way my Realm's Wall measures everything — by the *ending.* Did you win? Did the wound close? Is the account *settled?* That's the Wall's whole cruel arithmetic: it asks how a thing *ended*, what's *owed*, whether the books *balance* — and by that math, forty years of holding a line that's still un-won looks like a loss. But that math is asking the wrong question, Iona. The wound never closing doesn't *un-buy* a single one of those days you bought. Every dawn an ordinary soul woke up safe and never knew why — *that* happened. That's real, and permanent, and nothing about how the story ends reaches back and unmakes it. You weren't fighting to *close* the wound. You were fighting to *hold* it, one day at a time, and you *held* it, every day, for forty years. The holding *was* the victory. It was never deferred to some ending that never came — it was *complete*, every single day, in the holding itself. The Wall asks 'did you win.' The right question is 'did you hold the line today' — and the answer, for fourteen thousand days, was *yes.*"
"Tell me about them, Iona — the ones you held it beside. A soldier doesn't smile like that about a *cause.* That smile's about *people.*"
"Keep the fire warm, Iona. I'll come back before I walk to my own wall."

nodes.speaker: Iona
nodes.text: *(She sits easy under your regard, unguarded as only the truly settled can be, and you let your sense move past the contented surface to what four decades on an unwinnable wall actually forged.)* *(And the wonder of it is that your sense finds no rot. By every law you have learned in this City, there should be rot — futility is a slow poison, and a soul that spends forty years on a fight it cannot win should be hollowed by it, gone bitter or gone numb. Iona is neither. Your sense reads how she escaped it, and it is a kind of genius, though she would laugh to hear it called that: she simply *refused the long view.* She never once let herself measure the work by the wound, by the ending, by the un-winnable whole — because she understood, early, that to measure an endless holding-action by its endlessness is to guarantee despair. Instead she measured it by the *day.* Did the line hold *today.* Were the people safe *tonight.* And the day, your sense reads, was always winnable, was won again and again, fourteen thousand small complete victories strung on forty years, none of them deferred, none of them owed, each one *finished* the moment the sun set on a wall still held. Your sense reads, too, the thing that fed her where the cause alone could not: the company. Not the abstraction of the crusade but the specific, beloved, exasperating, mortal people beside her in the gap — and reads that she did not, in the end, hold the line *for* the cause at all. She held it for the souls on her left and right, and they held it for her, and the holding-each-other was the actual substance of the thing, the cause merely the place it happened to occur. And your sense reads the one shadow, the question she carries even now: a small, stubborn fear, planted by no one but the sheer arithmetic of an un-won war, that maybe it didn't *count* — that a victory with no final triumph at the end of it might not have been a victory at all. Your sense reads that she is wrong about that, and that she half-knows she is wrong, and that she has been waiting, by this fire, for someone to come along who could tell her so in a way she could finally believe.)*

nodes.speaker: Iona
nodes.text: *(She's quiet a moment, and the easy warmth doesn't leave her face, but it deepens, goes more honest, the way a person's does when they decide to actually say the thing.)* …The question. *(she turns the clean sword over once in her hands)* …It's a foolish one, traveler, for a woman who claims to be at peace, and I'm a little ashamed to carry it. But you asked straight, so I'll answer straight. *(quietly)* …The wound never closed. …I gave it forty years — my youth, my strength, near everyone I loved, the *whole* of me — and when I died on that wall, the wound was *exactly* as open as the day I first stood my watch. *(a breath)* …And sometimes, traveler, in the quiet by this fire, the small mean voice gets in, the one that does *sums*, and it says: *'Then what was it for? You held a door that's still being held by someone else now, and will be by someone else after them, world without end, and the dark's no further back than when you started. You didn't* win *anything, Iona. You just* delayed. *Forty years of delaying.'* *(she looks at the fire)* …And I *know* it's the wrong voice. I *knew* the work was holy when I did it, I never once doubted it on the wall itself. …But the wall's behind me now, and there's time to *think*, and thinking's a great enemy of a contented soul. *(she meets your eyes, and there's the faint plea in them)* …So that's my question, traveler. The one I'd set down if I could. …*Does a victory count, if it never ends in a triumph? …Did the holding mean anything …if the thing I held is still, right now, un-won?*

nodes.speaker: Iona
nodes.text: *(She goes still, the sword forgotten in her hands, and as you speak you watch forty years' worth of a quiet, stubborn ache simply… ease, the way a long-clenched muscle lets go when someone finally presses the right place. By the end her eyes are bright, and she is laughing, softly, at herself.)* …*'The holding was the victory.'* *(she shakes her head, wondering)* …*'It was never deferred to an ending that never came.'* *(she presses a scarred hand to the sunburst on her chest)* …Traveler. Forty years, and I never once turned it the right way up. *(her voice fills, warm and amazed)* …I kept *grading* myself against the *wound* — against the *whole*, the *ending*, the *did-you-finally-win* — and *of course* that came up *short*, because the wound was *never* a thing one lifetime *could* close; it was a thing you *held*, and holding was the *whole* of the *job*, and I *did* the *whole* of the *job*, *every* *single* *day.* *(she laughs, wet and delighted)* …Fourteen thousand days. Fourteen thousand *complete* *victories.* Not *one* of them *owed* its *meaning* to some *triumph* down the road — each one *paid* in *full* the *moment* the *sun* *set* on a *line* still *held.* *(and then her face changes, the soldier coming back into it, fierce and clear)* …And your *Wall*, traveler. *(she stands, sword in hand, the crusader entire)* …Now I see what your Wall *is.* It is the *wrong* *voice*, made into *law.* It asks *every* soul the *sum* — *how did you* end, *what are you* owed, *did the books* balance — and any soul whose *worth* was in the *holding* and not the *winning*, in the *journey* and not the *arrival*, comes up *short* by a *math* that was *never* the *right* math to begin with. *(she sets her jaw)* …*Go* and ask them the *right* question, traveler — every soul mortared in that wall for not having *ended* *grandly* *enough*, for not having *won*, for not having *balanced.* …Don't ask them *how did it end.* …Ask them *did you hold your line, on the days you were given.* …And I will *wager* my *forty* *years* …that *most* of them, the *small* and the *unwon* and the *written-off* …answer *yes.* *(she grins, fierce and free)* …The Wall has been *grading* the *wrong* *exam*, traveler. …*Go* and *tell* the *failures* …they *passed.*

nodes.speaker: Iona
nodes.text: *(And there it is — the whole face opening like a window, the grief and the joy of it inseparable, the look of someone invited to talk about the people they love.)* …*Them.* *(softly, fond)* …Oh, you've a sharp eye, traveler. You're right. The *cause* I respected. The *people* I *loved.* *(she settles back, smiling at the fire)* …There was Berrin, who held the spot on my left for *nineteen* *years* and never *once* told a story the same way twice — by the end the same battle had him fighting *forty* demons *single-handed*, and we let him, because the *lie* got *better* every *telling* and the *truth* was only that we'd *all* been *terrified* together. *(she laughs)* …There was little Ash, who came to us a *frightened* *farm-girl* and left us — well, she *didn't* leave us; she *died* on the wall at *twenty-six*, holding *my* spot when I was *down* with fever, and I have *never* stopped *owing* her, and I *carry* her *name* to *every* fire I sit at, *including* this one. *(quietly)* …That's the thing nobody tells you about a *wall*, traveler. …You think you're going to hold it for the *cause*, for the *people-behind*, for the *holy* *idea* of it. …And then you *get* there, and the *idea* is *cold* comfort at *three* in the *morning* with the *horrors* *howling* — and what's *warm*, what's *actually* *warm*, what gets you to *stand* *up* and do it *again*, is *Berrin's* *stupid* *grin* on your *left* and *Ash's* *steady* *hands* on your *right.* *(she meets your eyes)* …I didn't hold that line for *forty* *years* for the *cause*, traveler. *(barely, smiling)* …I held it because I *couldn't* *bear* to let *Berrin* and *Ash* stand there *without* me. …The *cause* was just the *place.* …The *people* were the *reason.* …They *always* are.*

nodes.speaker: Iona
nodes.text: *(She nods, and claps you once on the shoulder, a soldier's benediction, warm and solid.)* …Go on, then. *(she smiles)* …And come *back* before you walk to that grey wall of yours — I mean it. There's a thing I want to send with you, and I'll have it ready. *(she nods across the fire)* …See the others while you're here. The *wanderer*, Dalka — there's a soul who measured a life by the *road* instead of the *destination*, same as I learned to measure mine by the *day*; you'll find we rhyme. *(and then her weathered face splits into a helpless grin)* …And the little *green* one on the *wagon.* *Squik.* *(she laughs)* …Don't you *dare* walk past Squik, traveler. I *know* what your kind *thinks* a goblin is — *vermin*, *monster*, a *thing* you *clear* out of the *first* *room.* …*Sit* down and let it *sing* you its *song.* *(quietly, still smiling)* …That little creature taught me something, at the *end* of my *forty* *serious* *holy* *years*, that all my *crusading* never did. …*Go* find out *what.* …It's the *best* thing in this whole resting-place, traveler. …A soul that was *never* *anything* *but* *exactly* *itself* …and found that was *more* than *enough.*

variants.text: *(The goblin bounces on the wagon-seat, ears flapping, delighted you've stayed, and launches straight back in as though no time has passed.)* …Okay okay okay so FIRST you have to understand about Squik. *(it holds up a clawed finger, very serious)* …Squik is a GOBLIN. A real one! Not a — not a *secretly-a-prince* goblin, not a *cursed-nobleman* goblin, not a goblin with a *sad* *backstory* that makes the longshanks go *awww.* *(it shakes its head firmly)* …Squik LIKES fire. Squik likes it a LOT. Squik bit people, when Squik was alive — bit them right on the leg, it was GREAT. Squik is scared of dogs, ALL dogs, even small fluffy ones, ESPECIALLY small fluffy ones, they are CLEARLY plotting. Squik cannot read and thinks reading is a longshanks trick to make their eyes tired. *(it beams, utterly without shame)* …Squik is, in every single way, EXACTLY the dumb little monster you thought Squik was when you walked up. *(then it tilts its head, and the mad bright eyes get unexpectedly sharp)* …And here is the part that makes the longshanks go all *quiet* and *confused*, cold one: …Squik is also COMPLETELY HAPPY. …Was happy ALIVE. Is happy DEAD. …Squik had a GOOD little life, full of fire and biting and one — *(the voice goes soft, that surprising shyness again)* …one really, really good FRIEND. …And Squik does not understand why everybody else here has such SAD eyes. …Is being a person supposed to HURT this much, cold one? …Because Squik did it WRONG, then. …Squik had a GREAT time.*

nodes.speaker: Squik
nodes.text: *(It plops down cross-legged on the wagon-seat, chin in its claws, regarding you with bright expectant glee, ready to be asked things, which it clearly considers a tremendous treat.)* …Ask Squik stuff! *(it wriggles)* …Squik LOVES getting asked stuff. The serious ones, the knight and the walker, they always want to *tell* Squik things, big *important* things, and Squik *nods* and Squik *listens* but secretly Squik is thinking about fire. *(it grins)* …But YOU can ask SQUIK. …What do you wanna know, cold one?

choices.text: *(read the goblin — past the dumb-little-monster act, to whether the happiness is actually real)* "Hold still a second, Squik. Let me really look at you — see if all this happy is the real thing or just a loud noise you make."
"Why are you here, Squik? This is a city of legends — knights and archmages and heroes of a hundred worlds. What's a happy little goblin doing in the middle of all of it?"

*(sing it with him — really commit, no condescension, all the way: the fire, the biting, the heads, and the friend part)* "All right, Squik. Sing me the song — the whole thing, fire and biting and heads and all. And I'm not going to stand here and *politely* listen. I'm going to sing it *with* you. Teach me the chorus. Let's wake up this whole solemn city."

*(the one who knows joy is its own worth — that you don't have to be a tragedy to matter)* "Squik — listen, because you've got something every other soul in this city is dying to remember, and you don't even know it's special. You keep apologizing, basically, for being happy — for not having the sad eyes, for having had a *great time* being exactly a goblin. Stop. You did it *right*, Squik, and everyone else's sad eyes don't mean you did it wrong; it means *they* forgot the thing you never lost. Here it is: a soul doesn't have to be a *tragedy* to be *worth* something. You weren't secretly a prince. You didn't have a noble destiny. You were a little green monster who loved fire and feared dogs and had one good friend — and you were *completely, uncomplicatedly happy*, and that happiness was *real*, and it *counts.* My Realm's Wall would look at you and see *nothing* — no grand deeds, no tragic worth, no debt anyone owes you, just a vermin-thing that had a nice time and died. And the Wall would be *wrong*, because a soul that was simply, fully *glad to be alive* — that loved its small life with its whole small heart — is carrying something most of the legends in this city would trade their glory for. You don't need a sad story to matter, Squik. You mattered because you were *happy*, and you were *yourself*, and you *loved your friend.* That was always enough. It was *more* than enough."
"Keep singing, Squik. I'll be back — and next time I want to hear the friend part."

nodes.speaker: Squik
nodes.text: *(It holds still — for about two seconds, which for Squik is an eternity of solemnity — and lets you look, and you let your sense reach past the bouncing and the grin to whatever is actually underneath.)* *(And the joy is real. That is the first thing your sense finds and it is almost startling in this city of the grieving: there is no rot under Squik, no secret wound, no tragedy the brightness is papering over. Your sense braces, out of long habit in these doors, for the sad truth beneath the happy act — and finds, instead, that the happy act is not an act, and there is no sad truth. Squik was a goblin, and lived a goblin's short rough life — hungry sometimes, hunted sometimes, knee-deep in the casual violence that is simply the weather of a goblin's world — and your sense reads that none of it ever once curdled the creature's bone-deep, uncomplicated gladness at being *alive*, at fire being *warm* and *bright*, at biting being *fun*, at the world being a place full of things to *do.* Your sense reads a soul entirely without the machinery of self-pity, that never learned to measure its life against what it lacked because it was too busy delighting in what it had. And then, woven all through the bright simple fabric of it, your sense finds the one thread that is different — softer, deeper, the thing the goblin gets shy about: the friend. A specific warmth, a someone, your sense cannot tell if it was another goblin or a longshank or some stray dog Squik somehow, against all goblin sense, stopped being afraid of — but a real and central and treasured bond, the one thing in Squik's whole gleeful existence that it held *gently.* And your sense reads the truth that the whole solemn city around this wagon has forgotten: that this small green creature, which every legend here would have stepped over without a glance, achieved the thing the archmages and the crusaders and the deathless heroes mostly *didn't* — it was *happy*, simply and wholly and to the end, and it *loved someone*, and it *knew*, in its own daft way, that those two things were the entire point. Squik did not have a small life, your sense reads. It had a *complete* one. It just came in a small green package that the world was too proud to open.)*

nodes.speaker: Squik
nodes.text: *(It scratches behind one enormous ear, thinking about it, and for once the answer comes slow, like it's a thing Squik has actually wondered too.)* …Why is Squik HERE. *(it frowns)* …Yeah. Squik thought about that. The big door-lady, the Dustman one, when Squik first wandered in, she looked at Squik like Squik was a — like a *clerical error.* Like Squik had snuck in. *(it grins)* …Maybe Squik DID sneak in! Squik is VERY good at sneaking. …But here's what Squik figured out, cold one, sitting on this nice wagon watching all the sad-eyed heroes go by. *(it leans forward)* …All the OTHERS here came because they're STUCK. Right? The knight-lady's got her question, the gold-skin mage down the OTHER doors has his whole — *(it waves a claw)* …his whole *thing*, everybody's *carrying* something, everybody's got a *knot* that needs *untying.* *(it taps its chest)* …Squik hasn't got a knot. Squik checked. *(beaming)* …So Squik thinks maybe Squik isn't here to get *fixed*, cold one. …Squik thinks maybe Squik is here to be — *(it hunts for the word, proud when it lands)* …*EVIDENCE.* …That's a longshanks word Squik learned. EVIDENCE. *(it nods hard)* …Squik is here so that when a sad cold one like you comes walking through, all worried about whether your little life MATTERED, whether you were GRAND enough, whether you DID enough — …you can look over and see a dumb happy goblin who did NOTHING grand, who was JUST a goblin, who had a GREAT time and loved a friend — …and Squik is sitting right HERE, in the city of LEGENDS, *(it spreads its arms wide)* …which means Squik COUNTS too. …Which means maybe YOU don't have to be grand either, cold one. …Maybe you just had to be HERE. …And HAPPY. …And somebody's FRIEND. …Squik thinks that's the job. …Squik thinks Squik is the EVIDENCE.*

nodes.speaker: Squik
nodes.text: *(You don't perform AT the goblin and you don't perform a charity-kindness FOR it — you throw yourself in beside it, learn the daft chorus, butcher the tune with total commitment, and bellow the fire-and-biting-and-heads parts loud enough to scandalize a city of legends. Squik's eyes go enormous. And then the two of you are SINGING, full-throated, a cold dead thing and a green dead goblin, and it is glorious, and it is ridiculous, and heads are turning all across the resting-place.)* *(Squik conducts you through the verses with both clawed arms, shrieking with joy, and at the end — the friend part — it doesn't get shy this time, because it isn't singing it alone, and it lets the soft tune ring out clear and true and unembarrassed, and somewhere across the fire you'd swear you see the old crusader wipe her eyes and grin.)* …*(When it's done Squik is breathless, beaming, incandescent.)* …YOU. *(it points at you, overcome)* …You did the HEADS part with your WHOLE CHEST. *(it flings itself up to standing on the wagon-seat)* …And you did not — cold one, you did not do the thing the OTHERS do, the *nice* thing, where they sing along *quiet* and *careful* like they're being *kind* to the *funny* little goblin. *(its mad eyes shine)* …You sang it like it was a GOOD SONG. …Because it IS a good song! *(fiercely happy)* …And the FRIEND part — you let Squik do the friend part LOUD, in front of EVERYBODY, and nobody laughed, because you'd made the whole song so BIG and so REAL that the friend part just — fit. *(it presses both claws to its chest)* …Cold one. …That is the nicest thing anybody ever did for Squik, alive OR dead. …You didn't *tolerate* Squik's song. …You *MEANT* it. …You treated a goblin's silly happy song …like it was worth SINGING. *(barely, glowing)* …That's all Squik ever wanted, you know. …Not to be FIXED. …Not to be PITIED. …Just — once — for a longshanks to sing the dumb goblin song …like they were GLAD it existed. …*Thank* you, cold one. …Squik will sing the FRIEND part about YOU now. …You're in the song forever. …That's the highest thing Squik's got to give.*

nodes.speaker: Squik
nodes.text: *(You sing along, gamely, and you keep up, and Squik is delighted — it conducts you through the fire and the biting and even gets you bellowing the heads part with a passable roar — and for a few bright bars the solemn resting-place rings with a goblin war-chant and a cold one's best effort.)* …HA! *(Squik claps)* …Not bad, cold one, not bad! You got the HEADS part almost right — it's more of a — *(it demonstrates, a gleeful shriek)* …like THAT, but you'll get it. *(it grins, pleased)* …Most longshanks won't even TRY. They go all *stiff*, or they sing it *quiet* like they're embarrassed FOR Squik. …You actually gave it a go, with your whole face. *(it nods approvingly)* …Squik likes you. *(then, a touch wistful)* …Come back and Squik'll teach you the FRIEND part too — that one's harder, it's not about fire OR biting, it's *quiet*, and you have to MEAN it, and Squik only teaches it to people who'd sing it *right.* *(it eyes you, hopeful)* …You might be people who'd sing it right, cold one. …Squik's gonna find out.*

nodes.speaker: Squik
nodes.text: *(You give the song a go — but you can't quite let go of yourself, and it comes out careful, self-conscious, a grown soul humoring a goblin, and Squik's sharp little ears catch the difference instantly. It doesn't get angry. It just deflates a little, and finishes the verse for both of you, gently, like it's used to this.)* …'S okay, cold one. *(a small shrug)* …You tried. That's more than most. *(it pats the wagon-seat)* …You went all *polite*, though. Squik could hear it. You were singing it like — like a *favor.* Like *'aw, look at the little goblin, let's make it happy.'* *(it isn't hurt, exactly, just matter-of-fact)* …And that's — that's *nice*, Squik guesses, but it's not the same as *meaning* it. When you sing it like a *favor*, cold one, it stops being a *song* and starts being a *kindness*, and a *kindness* is a thing you do *down* at somebody. *(it looks at you, bright and clear)* …Squik doesn't want to be *sung-down-at.* …Squik wants somebody to think the song is actually GOOD. …Come back when you can sing it like you're not doing Squik a *favor*, okay? …Like you're just two idiots singing about fire because fire's GREAT. …THAT one Squik'll treasure. …The favor one Squik's had plenty of.*

nodes.speaker: Squik
nodes.text: *(It goes wrong in a way you don't see coming — you lean into the *bit* of it, hamming the goblin-voice, mugging the dumb-little-monster shtick for a laugh — and you get the laugh, from a few souls nearby, but it's the wrong laugh, it's *at*, and Squik hears exactly what kind of laugh it is. The grin drops off its green face for the first time since you arrived.)* …Oh. *(quietly)* …You're doing the — you're doing the *voice.* *(it sits back down, small)* …The funny-goblin voice. To make the longshanks laugh. *(it picks at the wagon-seat)* …Squik knows that one too, cold one. Squik knows that one REAL well. That's the one where Squik isn't *singing* with you — Squik is the *joke* you're singing. *(it doesn't look at you)* …Squik thought you were gonna sing it *with* Squik. Like it was *ours.* …But you made Squik into the *punchline*, and now those longshanks over there are laughing at the *silly* *monster*, and Squik is — *(its voice gets very small)* …Squik is right back to being the thing in the first room that you clear out for a *chuckle.* *(a long pause, then, bravely, the grin coming back crooked)* …'S fine. Squik's used to it. …But hey — cold one? …Next time? …Don't do the *voice.* …Squik's voice is just Squik's *voice.* …It's not a *bit.* …Come back and sing it like Squik's a *person* singing a *song* …and not a *joke* you're *telling.* …Squik would like that a lot better. …Squik would like that more than almost anything.*

nodes.speaker: Squik
nodes.text: *(The goblin goes very still on its wagon-seat — really still, the whole bouncing engine of it stopping — and it stares at you with its bright mad eyes slowly filling, and when it speaks the reedy voice has lost all its performance and is just small, and real, and astonished.)* …*'You don't need a sad story to matter.'* *(barely)* …Cold one. …Squik has to tell you a secret. *(it scrubs at its eyes with the back of a claw)* …Squik *acts* like Squik doesn't notice. The sad eyes, the way the heroes look *past* Squik, the way the door-lady counted Squik a *mistake.* Squik bounces around and sings and pretends Squik is too *happy* and too *dumb* to clock it. *(it sniffs)* …But Squik clocks it, cold one. Squik *always* clocked it. …Every single soul in this whole grand city looked at Squik and thought the same thing: *'nice little creature, shame it doesn't really COUNT, not like US, not like the ones with the GRAND stories.'* *(its voice wobbles)* …And a little part of Squik, way down under the fire and the biting, started to *believe* it. That maybe being *happy* wasn't *enough.* That maybe you had to *suffer* to be *real.* That Squik's good little life was — was somehow *less*, because nothing in it ever *hurt* enough to make a *legend* out of.* *(and then it looks at you, blazing, the tears spilling over into a grin)* …And you just — you just *told* the whole city it had it *backwards.* …That Squik's happy little life isn't the thing that *doesn't* count — it's the thing everybody *else* is in this city *trying to remember how to have!* *(it leaps up, electric, joy and tears together)* …And your WALL, cold one! *(it points a claw, fierce)* …Squik gets it now! Your wall is the door-lady's *face*, made into a whole WALL! It looks at the happy ones, the small ones, the ones who just had a NICE TIME and loved a FRIEND, and it goes *'mistake, doesn't count, nothing owed, nothing grand'* — and it MORTARS them in for the crime of not SUFFERING IMPRESSIVELY ENOUGH! *(it stamps a little foot)* …*GO* tell them, cold one! Every happy little nothing-soul stuck in that wall! …*'You did it RIGHT! You don't need a sad story! Being glad and being somebody's friend was ALWAYS enough!'* *(it beams through the tears)* …And tell 'em SQUIK said so. …The EVIDENCE said so. …Now go on — *(it wipes its face, fierce and happy)* …go be GRAND at your wall by telling everybody they don't HAVE to be. …That's the most goblin thing Squik ever heard. …Squik LOVES it.*

nodes.speaker: Squik
nodes.text: *(It flops back onto the wagon-seat and gives you a cheerful wave, already half back into its endless song.)* …Bye, cold one! Go see the others! *(it points across the fire toward Dalka)* …The WALKER one is good, Squik likes the walker, the walker tells Squik about places with NO DOGS, which Squik is pretty sure is a lie but a NICE lie. *(it grins)* …And go see the knight-lady again, she pretends to be all *serious* but she sang the heads part with Squik ONE time and she was GOOD at it, she's got a whole secret loud goblin inside her serious knight outside. *(then it points a claw at you, mock-stern, eyes twinkling)* …But you come BACK, cold one. …'Cause you owe Squik a proper sing — *(it softens, that shy thing again, but braver now)* …and Squik's gonna teach you the FRIEND part. …The real one. …Squik decided. …You're people who'd sing it right. …Squik can tell. …Squik can ALWAYS tell. *(it beams)* …Go on! Bye! Don't get bit by a dog! …Oh — you can't, you're already dead. …LUCKY. *(it cackles, delighted, and goes back to singing.)*

variants.text: *(The wanderer makes a small welcoming gesture with the patched cloak, inviting you into the warmth, and there is in the gesture the unhurried hospitality of someone who has been a stranger in a thousand places and learned the grammar of welcome in all of them.)* …Settle in. *(a sun-creased smile)* …I'm Dalka. I'll save you the guessing about what I was: nothing. *(a warm chuckle)* …Truly. No order, no oath, no crusade, no grand purpose. Where the knight-lady held a wall and the little green one held a song, I held — *(spreads patched hands)* …nothing. A pack and a cloak and two good feet, most of my life. I was a *wanderer*, traveler. Left home at sixteen, not running from a single thing — good family, good town, nothing to flee — just *pulled.* There was always a next ridge, you see, and I needed, the way some folk need air, to know what was *over* it. *(the smile deepens, goes a little distant)* …And then there was a ridge past *that* one, and a coast past *that*, and a sea, and another land entire, and — well. Sixty years of next-ridges, traveler. I saw the wide world. Near the whole of it, as much as one life and two feet could cover. *(meets your eyes)* …And folk always asked me, all my life, the same question, with the same little frown — *'but what are you* doing *it* for, *Dalka? What's the* point? *Where are you* going?' *(softly)* …Sit a while, traveler. …Because I worked out the answer, eventually. …And it's a good one. …And I think it might be exactly the thing a soul walking toward a hard wall needs to hear.*

nodes.speaker: Dalka
nodes.text: *(The wanderer threads a needle by firelight with the steady hands of long practice, and settles in, the very picture of a soul with all the time in the world and a story for every minute of it.)* …Go on and ask. *(a warm glance)* …I've crossed too much country to be shy, and there's no question a body can put to me I haven't already been asked by some innkeeper or border-guard or curious child in sixty years of roads. …What'll it be?

choices.text: *(read the wanderer — past the easy traveller's charm, to what sixty years of next-ridges actually made)* "Let me look at you a moment, Dalka. A whole life on the road — I'd see what it built in a soul, all that going."
"What *was* it for, Dalka? The frown-question. Sixty years of walking with no destination — what's the answer you worked out?"

*(the one who knows the journey was the worth, and the Wall only ever asks about the destination)* "Dalka — I think you already know the answer, and I think you've been waiting at this fire to hear a stranger say it back so you know it's true. The frown-people asked *where are you going* because they could only imagine a journey being worth the destination at the end of it. But you found the thing they couldn't: that there is no there. There was never going to be a there. The *going* was the whole of it — the seeing, the crossing, the thousand horizons, the hundred ways to say hello — and not one mile of it was a means to some end down the road, because every mile was *already* the end, already complete, already the point, the moment your feet were on it. And here's why it matters to me, walking toward what I'm walking toward: my Realm has a Wall that judges every soul by exactly the frown-people's question. *Where did you end up? What did you arrive at? What's the destination, the final sum, the account at the close?* And a soul whose whole worth was in the *journey* — in the road walked and the wonders seen and the strangers greeted, none of it cashing out to any grand arrival — that soul fails the Wall's question completely, because the Wall is asking *where did you get to*, and your answer, the true and beautiful answer, is *nowhere — I was too busy going.* You didn't waste sixty years failing to arrive, Dalka. You spent sixty years *arriving constantly*, every step, and the Wall is too fixated on destinations to see that the journey was the destination all along."
"Tell me one, Dalka. One place. The best thing you ever saw out there, in sixty years of horizons. Give me a wonder to carry to my own hard road."
"Keep the road warm, Dalka. I'll come back — I've got a horizon of my own to walk first."

nodes.speaker: Dalka
nodes.text: *(The wanderer keeps stitching, unbothered, and you let your sense move past the easy weathered charm to what six decades of roads laid down in a soul.)* *(And what your sense finds is *width.* That is the only word for it: where another soul might be deep in one place, rooted, concentrated, Dalka is *wide* — spread out across a whole world, made of a thousand places and a thousand passing souls, a self assembled like the patched cloak from scraps of everywhere. Your sense reads no shallowness in it, though the frown-people always assumed there must be; it reads the opposite, a soul that learned more ways of being human than any settled life could teach, that carries inside it the particular quality of light in a hundred countries and the specific kindness of a hundred strangers who took a wanderer in for a night. And your sense reads the engine of it, the next-ridge hunger, and understands it at last not as restlessness, not as running, not as the inability to be content that the frown-people diagnosed — but as a form of *love.* For the world. A vast, promiscuous, inexhaustible love for the sheer variousness of creation, a need to *see* it the way a devout soul needs to pray, because to Dalka the seeing *was* the prayer — every new horizon a small act of worship toward a world too big and too strange and too beautiful to be honored by staying still. Your sense reads that Dalka never owned anything, never built anything, never arrived anywhere, and was not poorer for it but *richer*, carrying at the end a wealth no settled soul could match: the whole wide world, witnessed, held in a single grateful mind. And it reads the one small uncertainty the wanderer carries even now — the frown-people's question, lodged like a burr after sixty years: a quiet wondering whether a life of pure *going*, with nothing built and nowhere reached, might have been somehow *self-indulgent*, a long beautiful selfishness, when set beside the knight's wall and the goblin's friend. Your sense reads that this is wrong, that the seeing was its own kind of faithfulness, a way of telling the world *I noticed you, all of you, I came all this way just to look* — and that Dalka is one stranger's affirmation away from finally setting the burr down.)*

nodes.speaker: Dalka
nodes.text: *(The wanderer chuckles, sets down the needle, and looks into the fire with the fond, far gaze of someone going back over a great deal of country.)* …What was it for. *(a warm breath)* …Sixty years I got that question, traveler, and for the first thirty or so I didn't have an answer, just a *shrug* and a grin and a *'reckon I'll know it when I get there.'* …Trouble was, I never *got* there. *(a soft laugh)* …Every place I reached turned out to have a *next* place leaning over its shoulder, and off I'd go. And somewhere in my fifties I had the small cold thought the frown-people wanted me to have: *'Dalka, you damn fool, you've spent your whole life* going *and you never once* arrived. *You've got nothing to show. No house, no land, no name carved anywhere. You* wasted *it, chasing ridges.'* *(she nods slowly)* …And I sat with that, traveler. One cold night on a mountain pass, I really sat with it. …And then I looked *up.* *(her voice warms)* …And the *sky* up there, traveler — at that altitude, that far from any lamp — the sky was so *thick* with stars it looked *solid*, looked like you could *climb* it, more stars than dark, a thing I had *never* seen in sixty years and would *never* have seen if I'd *stayed home* and *built* my *sensible* *house.* *(she meets your eyes)* …And it came to me, clean and simple: *the going was never for the getting-there.* …There *is* no there. There was never going to *be* a there. …The going was for the *sky on the mountain.* For the *thing over the next ridge that I'd never see if I stopped.* …The point of the journey, traveler — *(softly, certain)* …the point of the journey *was the journey.* …Every step was already the whole of it. …I didn't *fail* to arrive, all those years. …I *arrived*, traveler — *constantly* — *every* *single* *step.* …I just never *stopped* arriving long enough to mistake one place for the *point.* …*That's* the answer. …Took me thirty years and a skyful of stars to find it. …*The road was never the way to somewhere. The road was the somewhere.*

nodes.speaker: Dalka
nodes.text: *(The wanderer is quiet for a long moment, firelight moving on the weathered face, and then lets out a slow breath that seems to carry sixty years on it, and the last small burr of doubt goes out with it.)* …*'You were too busy going.'* *(a soft, wondering laugh)* …Traveler, you've said it better than I ever managed, and I had *thirty years and a mountain* to work it out. *(presses a patched sleeve to one eye, unashamed)* …*'Arriving constantly.'* *(quietly)* …That's the thing the frown-people could never *hear*, and the thing I half-feared they were right about, even at the end — that all my *going* was just a long, lovely way of *running*, of never *committing*, of never *building* the *house* and *standing* the *wall* and *doing* the *grown-up* *settled* *thing.* *(she shakes her head slowly)* …But you're right. I wasn't *failing* to arrive. There was *nothing* to arrive *at.* The whole *frame* was *wrong* — *theirs* and, on the cold nights, *mine.* *(her voice fills, warm and clear)* …And your *Wall*, traveler. *(she sits up, the easy charm sharpening into something keener)* …Oh, now I see your Wall *true.* It's the *frown*, traveler — the *'where are you* going, *what's the* point, *where did you* arrive' — *built* into *stone* and *handed* a *ledger.* It judges *every* soul by the *destination*, by the *arrival*, by *what you got to in the end* — and so it *cannot* *see*, it is *structurally blind to*, the soul whose whole worth was the *going.* *(fierce now, delighted)* …*Go* and tell them, traveler — every wanderer and seeker and unfinished soul in that wall, every one mortared in for not having *arrived* anywhere *grand* enough: …the Wall is asking *'where did you get to.'* …And the *true* answer, the *beautiful* one, is *'nowhere — and that was the whole point, because I was* arriving *every step of the way.'* *(she grins, and it is the grin of someone who has just been handed back sixty years)* …A *journey* doesn't *owe* its *worth* to a *destination*, traveler. …*Tell* the *Wall* that. …And then — *(her eyes twinkle)* …then *walk* through it, to your *own* next ridge, and don't you *dare* mistake even *that* grim grey wall for the *point.* …It's just *another* horizon. …And there's *always*, *always* …one more past it.*

nodes.speaker: Dalka
nodes.text: *(The wanderer's whole face lights, and she settles in with the relish of a soul handed the thing it loves best — a listener, and leave to tell.)* …*One.* *(a delighted groan)* …Traveler, you ask a soul who saw the *whole* *world* for the *one* *best* *thing* — that's a *cruelty*, that is — but I'll not dodge it, because the truth is there *is* one, there's always one your mind comes home to. *(she leans in, firelit)* …It wasn't the great wonders. I saw those — the falls so high the water turns to *mist* before it lands, the cities of *white* *towers*, the canyon you could fit a *kingdom* in. They were grand, and I'm glad I saw them, but they're not the one. *(softly)* …The one was *small.* …Far north, traveler, past where the maps gave up, there's a stretch of coast where the cold sea meets the cold sky, nothing there, no town, no soul for a hundred miles. And I came over the last dune at *dusk*, footsore and half-regretting the whole detour — and the *whole* *sky*, traveler, the *entire* *northern* *sky*, was on *fire.* *(her voice hushes)* …Not sunset. Something else. *Curtains* of *light* — green, and violet, and a red like a *live* *coal* — *hanging* in the *dark* and *moving*, *breathing*, *folding* and *unfolding* in *total* *silence*, over a black sea, with *no* one there to see it but *me* and a few *seabirds.* *(she meets your eyes)* …And I stood there, traveler, an old fool with sore feet at the top of the world, and I *wept*, because it was so *beautiful* and so *vast* and so *utterly* *indifferent* to whether anyone was *watching* — it would have *danced* there *whether* *I* *came* *or* *not* — and I had *walked* *sixty* *years* to be the *one* *pair* of *eyes* that *caught* it, *that* *night.* *(barely)* …*That's* the one, traveler. …Not because it was the grandest. …Because it taught me what all the *going* was *for*: …the world is *full* of *unspeakable* *beauty* that *no* one is *there* to *see.* …And a *wanderer* — *(softly, certain)* …a wanderer is just a soul who decided that *somebody* *should.* …That's the wonder I'll send with you, traveler. …When your road is *hardest*, you *remember*: …somewhere *north* of *everything*, the *sky* is *dancing* over a *black* *sea*, *right* *now*, for *no* *one.* …And the *world* is *worth* *walking* *toward* …if *only* to be the *eyes* that *catch* it.*

nodes.speaker: Dalka
nodes.text: *(The wanderer nods, and returns to the patched cloak, but the sun-creased smile follows you, full of road-wisdom and warmth.)* …Go well, traveler. *(a warm gesture)* …And mind — a parting gift, from one walker to another, costs me nothing and might serve you. *(quietly)* …When you reach that grey wall of yours — the hard one, the one you're carrying toward like a stone in the boot — *(she meets your eyes)* …*don't* let it be the *destination.* That's the trap, you see. A wall like that *wants* to be the *end* of your *story*, the *there* you finally *arrive* at, the *point* it was all *for.* *(she shakes her head)* …It isn't. It's a *ridge*, traveler. A *hard* one, a *high* one, a *grim* grey *ridge* — but a *ridge*, and ridges have only ever *one* purpose: …there's *something* on the *other* *side* of it that you can't *see* yet. *(she smiles)* …*Cross* it. Not to *win* it, not to *end* at it — *cross* it, the way I crossed *sixty* years of *ridges*, because the *going-over* is the *whole* of the *living*, and there is *always*, *always* …a *next* *horizon* past *even* the *worst* *wall.* *(she winks)* …Now go on. …Walk it like a wanderer, traveler. …And come *back* and tell *me* …what was on the *other* *side.* …That's the *one* horizon *I* never got to. …I'd *dearly* love to hear there's a *next* *ridge* …even past *death's* *grey* *wall.**
